title: About
text: About this blog

title: Replication of Angrist and Lavy (1999)
text:
How much does class size affect student learning? This question carries enormous policy weight: smaller classes require more teachers and more buildings, making class-size reduction one of the most expensive interventions available to school systems. If the effect on achievement is small, the money is better spent elsewhere.
The challenge is that a simple comparison of large and small classes is badly confounded. Schools serving wealthier, higher-achieving communities often have larger cohorts — meaning bigger classes can appear to produce better outcomes in raw data. Any honest estimate must find a source of variation in class size that is unrelated to student background.
Angrist and Lavy (1999) solve this with a natural experiment rooted in a 12th-century legal text. The medieval scholar Maimonides wrote that a teacher should instruct no more than 40 pupils. Israeli law codified this principle: once a grade cohort exceeds 40 students it must be split into two classes, at 81 into three, and so on. This creates sharp discontinuities in class size at enrollment counts of 40, 80, 120, and 160. Two schools with nearly identical enrollments — say, 40 vs. 41 students — end up with very different class sizes (≈40 vs. ≈21) for a reason that has nothing to do with school quality. Comparing outcomes across these cutoffs yields a causal estimate of class size on achievement. This research design is called a Regression Discontinuity Design (RDD).
The Maimonides-predicted class size is:
\[
f(n) = \frac{n}{\lfloor (n-1)/40 \rfloor + 1}
\]
where \(n\) is the grade enrollment count. This function serves as the instrument for actual class size.

title: Replication of Angrist and Lavy (1999)
text:
How much does class size affect student learning? This question carries enormous policy weight: smaller classes require more teachers and more buildings, making class-size reduction one of the most expensive interventions available to school systems. If the effect on achievement is small, the money is better spent elsewhere.
The challenge is that a simple comparison of large and small classes is badly confounded. Schools serving wealthier, higher-achieving communities often have larger cohorts — meaning bigger classes can appear to produce better outcomes in raw data. Any honest estimate must find a source of variation in class size that is unrelated to student background.
Angrist and Lavy (1999) solve this with a natural experiment rooted in a 12th-century legal text. The medieval scholar Maimonides wrote that a teacher should instruct no more than 40 pupils. Israeli law codified this principle: once a grade cohort exceeds 40 students it must be split into two classes, at 81 into three, and so on. This creates sharp discontinuities in class size at enrollment counts of 40, 80, 120, and 160. Two schools with nearly identical enrollments — say, 40 vs. 41 students — end up with very different class sizes (≈40 vs. ≈21) for a reason that has nothing to do with school quality. Comparing outcomes across these cutoffs yields a causal estimate of class size on achievement. This research design is called a Regression Discontinuity Design (RDD).

\]

title: Replication of Angrist and Lavy (1999)
text:
Main Analysis

Figure 1, Panel B — Actual vs. Predicted Class Size (4th Grade)
The figure below replicates Figure 1 Panel B from the paper. Each blue dot is the average actual class size for schools at a given enrollment count; the red line is the Maimonides rule. The sawtooth pattern is the heart of the identification strategy: at every multiple of 40, the rule forces a sharp drop in predicted class size, generating variation that is as good as randomly assigned.


Code
agg = df4.groupby('c_size').agg(
    avg_classize=('classize', 'mean'),
    func1=('func1', 'first')
).reset_index()

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 4.5))

ax.scatter(agg['c_size'], agg['avg_classize'], s=20, color='#2c7bb6',
           alpha=0.75, label='Average class size', zorder=3)
ax.plot(agg['c_size'], agg['func1'], color='#d7191c', linewidth=2,
        label="Maimonides' rule", zorder=4)

for cutoff in [40, 80, 120, 160, 200]:
    ax.axvline(cutoff, color='gray', linestyle='--', linewidth=0.8, alpha=0.5)

ax.set_xlabel('Grade enrollment', fontsize=11)
ax.set_ylabel('Class size', fontsize=11)
ax.set_title("Average Class Size and Maimonides' Rule by Enrollment - 4th Grade", fontsize=12)
ax.legend(fontsize=10)
ax.set_xlim(0, 230); ax.set_ylim(0, 55)
ax.grid(axis='y', alpha=0.3)
ax.spines[['top','right']].set_visible(False)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()









Actual class sizes closely track the rule, confirming that Maimonides’ rule is a strong first-stage predictor — a prerequisite for the RDD to work.



Table II, Columns 7 & 10 — Naive OLS (Biased Baseline)
Before exploiting the discontinuities, we run a straightforward OLS regression of test scores on actual class size with standard controls (share of disadvantaged students tipuach, total enrollment c_size). These columns correspond to 4th-grade reading and math in Table II of the paper.


Code
results = {}
for outcome, label in [('avgverb', 'Reading'), ('avgmath', 'Math')]:
    for spec, formula in [
        ('No controls',                    f'{outcome} ~ classize'),
        ('+ % disadvantaged',              f'{outcome} ~ classize + tipuach'),
        ('+ % disadvantaged + enrollment', f'{outcome} ~ classize + tipuach + c_size'),
    ]:
        m = smf.ols(formula, data=df4).fit()
        results[(label, spec)] = {
            'Coeff': round(m.params['classize'], 4),
            'SE':    round(m.bse['classize'],   4),
            'N':     int(m.nobs),
        }

tbl = pd.DataFrame(results).T
tbl.index.names = ['Outcome', 'Controls']
print(tbl.to_string())


                                         Coeff      SE       N
Outcome Controls                                              
Reading No controls                     0.1415  0.0277  2049.0
        + % disadvantaged              -0.0530  0.0241  2049.0
        + % disadvantaged + enrollment -0.0405  0.0287  2049.0
Math    No controls                     0.2206  0.0302  2049.0
        + % disadvantaged               0.0548  0.0284  2049.0
        + % disadvantaged + enrollment  0.0091  0.0338  2049.0


The raw coefficient on class size is positive for both subjects — bigger classes naively appear to boost scores. Adding controls flips the reading coefficient negative and shrinks math to near zero, but the estimates remain unstable. This volatility signals severe omitted variable bias: larger class sizes cluster in urban, wealthier schools with stronger student performance, masking the true negative effect.
Paper targets (no controls): reading ≈ 0.14, math ≈ 0.22. Our estimates match.



Table III, Panel A, Columns 7, 9, 11 — RDD Reduced-Form Estimates (4th Grade)
Now we replace actual class size with the Maimonides rule (func1) as the regressor. This reduced-form specification regresses outcomes directly on the instrument. Because func1 is determined mechanically by enrollment, it is uncorrelated with school-level confounders. All three columns are for 4th grade.


Code
rdd_rows = []
for col_label, outcome, formula in [
    ('Col 7 - Reading, + disadvantaged %',
     'avgverb', 'avgverb ~ func1 + tipuach'),
    ('Col 9 - Reading, + disadvantaged % + enrollment',
     'avgverb', 'avgverb ~ func1 + tipuach + c_size'),
    ('Col 11 - Math, + disadvantaged % + enrollment',
     'avgmath', 'avgmath ~ func1 + tipuach + c_size'),
]:
    m = smf.ols(formula, data=df4_rdd).fit()
    rdd_rows.append({
        'Column': col_label,
        'Maimonides Rule Coeff': round(m.params['func1'], 4),
        'Std. Error':            round(m.bse['func1'],   4),
        'N': int(m.nobs),
    })

print(pd.DataFrame(rdd_rows).set_index('Column').to_string())


                                                 Maimonides Rule Coeff  Std. Error     N
Column                                                                                  
Col 7 - Reading, + disadvantaged %                             -0.0849      0.0256  2049
Col 9 - Reading, + disadvantaged % + enrollment                -0.0890      0.0325  2049
Col 11 - Math, + disadvantaged % + enrollment                  -0.0333      0.0382  2049


All three reduced-form coefficients are negative: a larger class size as predicted by the rule lowers test scores. The effect is more pronounced for reading than for math.
Paper targets: col 7 ≈ −0.08, col 9 ≈ −0.09, col 11 ≈ −0.03. Our estimates match closely.

Note on standard errors: The paper uses Moulton-corrected SEs that account for within-school clustering. Our unadjusted SEs will be smaller — a known discrepancy documented in the replication files.




Naive vs. RDD: What Changed?
The table below puts the key naive OLS and RDD estimates side by side for the fully-controlled specification.


Code
compare_rows = []
for outcome, label in [('avgverb','Reading'), ('avgmath','Math')]:
    naive = smf.ols(f'{outcome} ~ classize + tipuach + c_size', data=df4).fit()
    rdd   = smf.ols(f'{outcome} ~ func1   + tipuach + c_size', data=df4_rdd).fit()
    compare_rows.append({
        'Outcome':          label,
        'Naive OLS':        round(naive.params['classize'], 4),
        'RDD Reduced Form': round(rdd.params['func1'],      4),
    })

print(pd.DataFrame(compare_rows).set_index('Outcome').to_string())


         Naive OLS  RDD Reduced Form
Outcome                             
Reading    -0.0405           -0.0890
Math        0.0091           -0.0333


The naive estimates are near zero; the RDD estimates are consistently negative. Even after controlling for observed covariates, the naive regression picks up residual confounding — wealthier schools tend to have larger enrollments and better outcomes, biasing the class-size coefficient upward. The Maimonides rule breaks this correlation by creating enrollment-driven jumps in class size that are unrelated to school quality.

title: Replication of Angrist and Lavy (1999)
text:
Something Additional

Visualizing the Discontinuity in Outcomes
If the RDD is valid, test scores should themselves show a jump near the enrollment cutoffs — students just above the cutoff end up in smaller classes (the rule kicks in) and should score higher. The chart below plots mean reading scores by enrollment bin, with a vertical line at the first cutoff of 40.


Code
df_plot = df4_rdd.copy()
df_plot['bin'] = (df_plot['c_size'] // 2) * 2   # 2-student bins

# Focus on enrollment 10-80 to show the first discontinuity clearly
sub  = df_plot[(df_plot['c_size'] >= 10) & (df_plot['c_size'] <= 80)]
agg2 = sub.groupby('bin').agg(
    mean_verb=('avgverb', 'mean'),
    n=('avgverb', 'count')
).reset_index()

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 4.5))

ax.scatter(agg2['bin'], agg2['mean_verb'],
           s=agg2['n'] * 3, color='#2c7bb6', alpha=0.7, zorder=3,
           label='Mean reading score (bubble size proportional to n classes)')
ax.axvline(40, color='#d7191c', linewidth=2, linestyle='--',
           label='Cutoff: enrollment = 40')

# Fit separate linear trends on each side
left  = agg2[agg2['bin'] <  40]
right = agg2[agg2['bin'] >= 40]
for side, color in [(left, '#4393c3'), (right, '#d6604d')]:
    if len(side) > 1:
        z      = np.polyfit(side['bin'], side['mean_verb'], 1)
        x_line = np.linspace(side['bin'].min(), side['bin'].max(), 100)
        ax.plot(x_line, np.polyval(z, x_line), color=color, linewidth=2, alpha=0.8)

ax.set_xlabel('Grade enrollment', fontsize=11)
ax.set_ylabel('Mean reading score', fontsize=11)
ax.set_title('Reading Scores Around the First Maimonides Cutoff (enrollment = 40)\n4th Grade',
             fontsize=12)
ax.legend(fontsize=10)
ax.grid(axis='y', alpha=0.3)
ax.spines[['top','right']].set_visible(False)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()









Schools just above the cutoff (enrollment 41-50) benefit from class sizes suddenly halved by the rule, and their reading scores are visibly higher than the left-side trend would predict. This jump is the visual signature of the RDD — it shows the reduced-form effect of the Maimonides rule on outcomes without any regression machinery.
This plot makes the causal logic intuitive: two schools separated by just one student in total enrollment end up with very different classroom experiences — and measurably different test scores — purely because of an 800-year-old administrative rule.

title: Key Drivers Analysis
text:
This analysis identifies which product/brand attributes most strongly drive overall customer satisfaction. We use six complementary methods to measure importance, each capturing a different facet of the driver–outcome relationship.
The dependent variable is satisfaction. The independent variables (drivers) are: trust, build, differs, easy, appealing, rewarding, popular, service, and impact.


Code
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from sklearn.linear_model import LinearRegression
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestRegressor
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn.metrics import r2_score
from itertools import combinations
from math import factorial
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings('ignore')

# Load data
df = pd.read_csv("data_for_drivers_analysis.csv")

y_col = 'satisfaction'
x_cols = ['trust', 'build', 'differs', 'easy', 'appealing',
          'rewarding', 'popular', 'service', 'impact']

X = df[x_cols].values
y = df[y_col].values

# Standardize predictors and outcome for regression-based methods
scaler = StandardScaler()
X_std = scaler.fit_transform(X)
y_std = (y - y.mean()) / y.std()

print(f"Dataset: {df.shape[0]} respondents, {len(x_cols)} drivers")
print(f"Satisfaction mean: {y.mean():.2f}, std: {y.std():.2f}")


Dataset: 2553 respondents, 9 drivers
Satisfaction mean: 3.39, std: 1.17

title: Key Drivers Analysis
text:
This analysis identifies which product/brand attributes most strongly drive overall customer satisfaction. We use six complementary methods to measure importance, each capturing a different facet of the driver–outcome relationship.
The dependent variable is satisfaction. The independent variables (drivers) are: trust, build, differs, easy, appealing, rewarding, popular, service, and impact.


Code
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from sklearn.linear_model import LinearRegression
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestRegressor
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn.metrics import r2_score
from itertools import combinations
from math import factorial
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings('ignore')

# Load data
df = pd.read_csv("data_for_drivers_analysis.csv")

y_col = 'satisfaction'
x_cols = ['trust', 'build', 'differs', 'easy', 'appealing',
          'rewarding', 'popular', 'service', 'impact']

X = df[x_cols].values
y = df[y_col].values

# Standardize predictors and outcome for regression-based methods
scaler = StandardScaler()
X_std = scaler.fit_transform(X)
y_std = (y - y.mean()) / y.std()

print(f"Dataset: {df.shape[0]} respondents, {len(x_cols)} drivers")
print(f"Satisfaction mean: {y.mean():.2f}, std: {y.std():.2f}")


Dataset: 2553 respondents, 9 drivers
Satisfaction mean: 3.39, std: 1.17

title: Key Drivers Analysis
text:
Methods

1. Pearson Correlation
Simple bivariate correlation between each driver and satisfaction. Easy to compute and interpret, but does not account for inter-correlations among drivers.


Code
pearson = df[x_cols].corrwith(df[y_col])





2. Standardized Regression Coefficients
Each predictor is standardized (mean = 0, sd = 1) before fitting OLS. Coefficients represent the change in satisfaction (in standard deviations) per one-SD change in the driver, holding all others constant. This controls for multicollinearity but can be unstable when predictors are highly correlated.


Code
reg_full = LinearRegression().fit(X_std, y_std)
r2_full = r2_score(y_std, reg_full.predict(X_std))
std_coefs = pd.Series(reg_full.coef_, index=x_cols)





3. Drop-One (Usefulness)
Incremental R² — the decrease in model fit when a single driver is removed. Measures the unique contribution of each driver beyond all others.
\[\text{Usefulness}_j = R^2_{\text{full}} - R^2_{-j}\]


Code
drop_one = {}
for i, col in enumerate(x_cols):
    X_drop = np.delete(X_std, i, axis=1)
    reg_drop = LinearRegression().fit(X_drop, y_std)
    r2_drop = r2_score(y_std, reg_drop.predict(X_drop))
    drop_one[col] = r2_full - r2_drop
drop_one = pd.Series(drop_one)





4. Shapley Values (Linear Regression, Custom Implementation)
Shapley values from cooperative game theory fairly allocate the total R² among predictors. Each variable’s Shapley value is its weighted average marginal contribution across all possible orderings of variables entering the model.
\[\phi_j = \sum_{S \subseteq N \setminus \{j\}} \frac{|S|!(|N|-|S|-1)!}{|N|!} \left[ v(S \cup \{j\}) - v(S) \right]\]
where \(v(S)\) is the R² from the model using predictor subset \(S\).
This is a custom implementation — no external Shapley package is used.


Code
n = len(x_cols)

def r2_subset(indices):
    if len(indices) == 0:
        return 0.0
    Xs = X_std[:, indices]
    reg_s = LinearRegression().fit(Xs, y_std)
    return r2_score(y_std, reg_s.predict(Xs))

shapley = {}
for i, col in enumerate(x_cols):
    others = [j for j in range(n) if j != i]
    sv = 0.0
    for size in range(n):
        for subset in combinations(others, size):
            weight = factorial(size) * factorial(n - size - 1) / factorial(n)
            sv += weight * (r2_subset(list(subset) + [i]) - r2_subset(list(subset)))
    shapley[col] = sv
shapley = pd.Series(shapley)





5. Johnson’s Relative Weights
Johnson’s method decomposes R² into parts attributable to each predictor using an orthogonalized basis — the predictors are rotated into uncorrelated components via eigenvector decomposition, regression coefficients are obtained in that space, and contributions are mapped back to the original variables. This handles multicollinearity gracefully.


Code
from numpy.linalg import eig

corr_XX = np.corrcoef(X_std.T)
eigenvalues, eigenvectors = eig(corr_XX)
eigenvalues = eigenvalues.real
eigenvectors = eigenvectors.real

Lam = np.diag(np.sqrt(eigenvalues))
Lam_inv = np.diag(1.0 / np.sqrt(eigenvalues))
Z = X_std @ eigenvectors @ Lam_inv  # orthogonal predictors

reg_z = LinearRegression().fit(Z, y_std)
beta_z = reg_z.coef_

# Map contributions back and normalize to R2
johnson_raw = (eigenvectors @ (Lam @ np.diag(beta_z**2)) @ eigenvectors.T).diagonal()
johnson_normalized = johnson_raw / johnson_raw.sum() * r2_full
johnson_normalized = pd.Series(johnson_normalized, index=x_cols)





6. Random Forest — Mean Decrease in Impurity
A Random Forest ensemble is fit and variable importance is measured as the mean decrease in node impurity (variance) across all trees when a variable is used for splitting. This captures non-linear and interaction effects.


Code
rf = RandomForestRegressor(n_estimators=500, random_state=42)
rf.fit(X, y)
rf_importance = pd.Series(rf.feature_importances_, index=x_cols)

title: Key Drivers Analysis
text:
Results Table


Code
results = pd.DataFrame({
    'Pearson r':           pearson,
    'Std. Reg. Coef.':     std_coefs,
    'Drop-One':            drop_one,
    'Shapley (LM, R²)':    shapley,
    "Johnson's RW":        johnson_normalized,
    'RF Impurity':         rf_importance,
})

# Sort by Shapley value descending (a balanced importance metric)
results = results.sort_values('Shapley (LM, R²)', ascending=False)

results.style \
    .format("{:.4f}") \
    .background_gradient(cmap='YlOrRd', axis=0) \
    .set_caption(f"Key Drivers of Satisfaction  |  Full-model R² = {r2_full:.4f}") \
    .set_table_styles([
        {'selector': 'caption', 'props': [('font-size', '14px'), ('font-weight', 'bold'), ('padding', '8px')]},
        {'selector': 'th', 'props': [('background-color', '#2c3e50'), ('color', 'white'), ('padding', '8px 12px')]},
        {'selector': 'td', 'props': [('padding', '6px 12px')]},
    ])







Table 1: Key Drivers of Satisfaction | Full-model R² = 0.1090

title: Key Drivers Analysis
text:
Interpretation


Code
print(f"Full-model R² = {r2_full:.4f}")
print(f"\nTop driver by Shapley: {shapley.idxmax()} ({shapley.max():.4f})")
print(f"Top driver by Pearson r: {pearson.idxmax()} ({pearson.max():.4f})")
print(f"Top driver by Std. Coef.: {std_coefs.idxmax()} ({std_coefs.max():.4f})")
print(f"Top driver by RF Impurity: {rf_importance.idxmax()} ({rf_importance.max():.4f})")


Full-model R² = 0.1090

Top driver by Shapley: impact (0.0236)
Top driver by Pearson r: trust (0.2557)
Top driver by Std. Coef.: impact (0.1284)
Top driver by RF Impurity: trust (0.1523)


The six methods tell a consistent story:

impact and trust emerge as the top two drivers across nearly all methods. Both show the highest Pearson correlations, the largest standardized regression coefficients, and the largest drop-one and Shapley values.
service is a consistent third-tier driver, appearing important across all methods.
Methods agree on the top drivers but diverge on the middle tier, which is expected — Pearson captures total association (including shared variance), standardized coefficients capture unique effects, and Shapley/Johnson/RF each handle multicollinearity differently.
Johnson’s relative weights distribute importance more evenly because they account for shared variance; rewarding, easy, and service rank relatively higher here than in uniqueness-focused metrics like drop-one.
The Random Forest ranks trust, impact, and service highest, consistent with the regression-based methods, and may capture mild non-linearities.
The overall R² of ~0.11 is modest, suggesting these binary attribute perceptions explain a meaningful but incomplete portion of satisfaction variance — other unmeasured factors (price, prior experience, etc.) also matter.

title: Does Word Choice Matter? Running an A/B Test on a Newsletter Sign-Up
text:
Imagine you run a small online publication and your homepage features a newsletter sign-up box. The box works, but you have a nagging feeling that the wording of the prompt — the “call to action” (CTA) — might matter more than you think. So you draft two versions:

CTA A: “Sign up for our newsletter here!”
CTA B: “Stay in the loop — join our newsletter today!”

Both say roughly the same thing, but do they convert visitors at the same rate? Intuition alone can’t answer that. Luckily, we can run an A/B test.
An A/B test is one of the simplest and most powerful tools in a data scientist’s toolkit. You randomly assign each visitor to see one of the two CTAs — half see A, half see B — then track who actually signs up. Because the assignment is random, any difference in sign-up rates between the two groups can be attributed to the wording itself rather than to some confounding factor (time of day, referral source, etc.).
In this post we won’t just run the test — we’ll use simulation to illuminate the statistical machinery underneath it: the Law of Large Numbers, the Bootstrap, the Central Limit Theorem, hypothesis testing, the equivalence of a t-test and a regression, and the danger of “peeking” at results before the experiment is finished.

title: Does Word Choice Matter? Running an A/B Test on a Newsletter Sign-Up
text:
Imagine you run a small online publication and your homepage features a newsletter sign-up box. The box works, but you have a nagging feeling that the wording of the prompt — the “call to action” (CTA) — might matter more than you think. So you draft two versions:

CTA A: “Sign up for our newsletter here!”
CTA B: “Stay in the loop — join our newsletter today!”

Both say roughly the same thing, but do they convert visitors at the same rate? Intuition alone can’t answer that. Luckily, we can run an A/B test.
An A/B test is one of the simplest and most powerful tools in a data scientist’s toolkit. You randomly assign each visitor to see one of the two CTAs — half see A, half see B — then track who actually signs up. Because the assignment is random, any difference in sign-up rates between the two groups can be attributed to the wording itself rather than to some confounding factor (time of day, referral source, etc.).
In this post we won’t just run the test — we’ll use simulation to illuminate the statistical machinery underneath it: the Law of Large Numbers, the Bootstrap, the Central Limit Theorem, hypothesis testing, the equivalence of a t-test and a regression, and the danger of “peeking” at results before the experiment is finished.

title: Does Word Choice Matter? Running an A/B Test on a Newsletter Sign-Up
section: The A/B Test as a Statistical Problem
text:
The A/B Test as a Statistical Problem
Every visitor who lands on our homepage either signs up (1) or doesn’t (0). That binary outcome is perfectly described by a Bernoulli distribution: a coin flip with probability \(\pi\) of coming up heads (signing up). Because the CTA a visitor sees might influence their decision, we allow the sign-up probability to differ across groups:

Visitors who see CTA A sign up with probability \(\pi_A\).
Visitors who see CTA B sign up with probability \(\pi_B\).

The quantity we actually care about is the difference in conversion rates:
\[\theta = \pi_A - \pi_B\]
If \(\theta > 0\), CTA A converts better; if \(\theta < 0\), CTA B is better; if \(\theta = 0\), the wording doesn’t matter.
We can’t observe \(\theta\) directly — we only ever see samples. Our best guess, called an estimator, is the difference in sample proportions:
\[\hat\theta = \bar{X}_A - \bar{X}_B\]
where \(\bar{X}_A\) is the fraction of CTA-A visitors who signed up, and \(\bar{X}_B\) is the fraction of CTA-B visitors who signed up. The rest of this post studies the properties of this estimator: when can we trust it, how precise is it, and when does it mislead us?

title: Does Word Choice Matter? Running an A/B Test on a Newsletter Sign-Up
text:
Simulating Data
In a real experiment we would never know the true values of \(\pi_A\) and \(\pi_B\). But for the purpose of this tutorial we get to play god: we set the true parameters ourselves so we can check whether our statistical tools recover the truth.
Let’s suppose:
\[\pi_A = 0.22, \quad \pi_B = 0.18, \quad \theta = 0.04\]
CTA A really is four percentage points better. We simulate 1,000 visitors assigned to each group — 2,000 total.


Code
pi_A = 0.22
pi_B = 0.18
n    = 1_000

A = rng.binomial(1, pi_A, n)   # 1 = signed up, 0 = did not
B = rng.binomial(1, pi_B, n)

theta_hat = A.mean() - B.mean()
print(f"Sample proportion (A): {A.mean():.4f}")
print(f"Sample proportion (B): {B.mean():.4f}")
print(f"Estimated difference θ̂: {theta_hat:.4f}  (true θ = 0.04)")


Sample proportion (A): 0.2240
Sample proportion (B): 0.1990
Estimated difference θ̂: 0.0250  (true θ = 0.04)


Our point estimate is already quite close to the true value of 0.04. But is that luck, or can we expect \(\hat\theta\) to reliably track \(\theta\)? That’s exactly the question the Law of Large Numbers answers.

title: Does Word Choice Matter? Running an A/B Test on a Newsletter Sign-Up
text:
The Law of Large Numbers
The Law of Large Numbers (LLN) states that as the sample size grows, the sample mean converges to the population mean. Applied here: as we collect more and more visitors, \(\bar{X}_A\) homes in on \(\pi_A\), \(\bar{X}_B\) homes in on \(\pi_B\), and therefore \(\hat\theta = \bar{X}_A - \bar{X}_B\) homes in on \(\theta = 0.04\).
The plot below shows this convergence in action. We take the 1,000 paired draws we already simulated, compute their element-wise differences, and track the running average of those differences as observations accumulate.


Code
diffs       = A - B
running_avg = np.cumsum(diffs) / np.arange(1, n + 1)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 4))
ax.plot(np.arange(1, n + 1), running_avg, color=BLUE, lw=1.5, label=r"Running $\hat\theta$")
ax.axhline(0.04, color=RED, ls="--", lw=1.5, label=r"True $\theta = 0.04$")
ax.axhline(0.00, color=GREY, ls=":", lw=1,   label="Null (no difference)")
ax.set_xlabel("Number of observations", fontsize=12)
ax.set_ylabel(r"Cumulative average of $X_A - X_B$", fontsize=12)
ax.set_title("Law of Large Numbers: Convergence of the Estimator", fontsize=13, fontweight="bold")
ax.legend(fontsize=11)
ax.set_xlim(1, n)
ax.yaxis.set_major_formatter(mticker.PercentFormatter(xmax=1, decimals=1))
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()





Running average of θ̂ as more observations arrive. The estimate starts noisy but converges to the true value of 0.04.




With only a handful of observations the running average bounces around wildly — at some points it even dips below zero, suggesting (falsely) that CTA B is better. But as the sample grows, the noise averages out and \(\hat\theta\) settles comfortably near the true value of 4%. This is why sample size matters so much in practice: the LLN gives us eventual accuracy, but we need a large enough \(n\) to get there.

title: Does Word Choice Matter? Running an A/B Test on a Newsletter Sign-Up
text:
Bootstrap Standard Errors
Knowing that \(\hat\theta\) will eventually be close to \(\theta\) is reassuring, but in practice we run an experiment once with a fixed sample size. The next question is: how precise is our single estimate? Can we quantify the uncertainty around \(\hat\theta\)?
The bootstrap is an elegant, assumption-light method for doing exactly this. The key idea is to treat our observed data as if it were the population, and ask: if we re-ran the experiment by sampling from our data (with replacement), how much would \(\hat\theta\) vary from resample to resample? That variability is our estimate of the standard error.
Here’s the procedure:


Code
B_boot = 1_000
boot_estimates = np.empty(B_boot)

for i in range(B_boot):
    A_star = rng.choice(A, size=n, replace=True)
    B_star = rng.choice(B, size=n, replace=True)
    boot_estimates[i] = A_star.mean() - B_star.mean()

se_boot = boot_estimates.std()

# Analytical SE
pi_A_hat = A.mean()
pi_B_hat = B.mean()
se_analytical = np.sqrt(pi_A_hat*(1-pi_A_hat)/n + pi_B_hat*(1-pi_B_hat)/n)

# 95% CI using bootstrap SE
ci_low  = theta_hat - 1.96 * se_boot
ci_high = theta_hat + 1.96 * se_boot

print(f"Bootstrap SE:   {se_boot:.5f}")
print(f"Analytical SE:  {se_analytical:.5f}")
print(f"95% CI for θ:  ({ci_low:.4f}, {ci_high:.4f})")


Bootstrap SE:   0.01859
Analytical SE:  0.01825
95% CI for θ:  (-0.0114, 0.0614)







Distribution of 1,000 bootstrap resamples of θ̂. The spread of this distribution is our estimate of the standard error.




The bootstrap standard error and the analytical formula give nearly identical answers — this is reassuring, because it confirms that the bootstrap is working as expected even though it never looks at the true parameters. The 95% confidence interval spans from roughly -0.011 to 0.061. A frequentist interpretation: if we repeated this whole procedure many times, 95% of the intervals constructed this way would contain the true \(\theta\). Crucially, the interval does not contain zero, which hints that our data favor CTA A — though we’ll make that formal in the hypothesis testing section.

title: Does Word Choice Matter? Running an A/B Test on a Newsletter Sign-Up
text:
The Central Limit Theorem
The Central Limit Theorem (CLT) is one of the most remarkable results in all of statistics. It says that no matter what shape the underlying population distribution takes, the sampling distribution of the sample mean becomes approximately Normal as the sample size grows. For us, this means that \(\hat\theta = \bar{X}_A - \bar{X}_B\) is approximately Normal even though the raw data are Bernoulli (binary), as long as \(n\) is large enough.
To see this in action, we repeat the experiment 1,000 times at four different sample sizes. For each repetition and each \(n\), we draw fresh data and compute a new \(\hat\theta\).


Code
sample_sizes = [25, 50, 100, 500]
sim_estimates = {}

for n_sim in sample_sizes:
    ests = np.empty(1_000)
    for i in range(1_000):
        a = rng.binomial(1, pi_A, n_sim)
        b = rng.binomial(1, pi_B, n_sim)
        ests[i] = a.mean() - b.mean()
    sim_estimates[n_sim] = ests







Sampling distribution of θ̂ at four sample sizes. As n grows, the distribution tightens and becomes more bell-shaped — the Central Limit Theorem in action.




At \(n = 25\) the histogram is lumpy and noticeably skewed — the Normal approximation is visibly rough. By \(n = 100\) the bell shape is already quite good, and by \(n = 500\) the simulated distribution is nearly indistinguishable from the theoretical Normal. This progression is the CLT in a picture: larger samples smooth out the quirks of the underlying Bernoulli distribution and reveal the universal bell curve underneath.

title: Does Word Choice Matter? Running an A/B Test on a Newsletter Sign-Up
text:
Hypothesis Testing
Now that we know \(\hat\theta\) is approximately Normal for large \(n\), we have everything we need to run a formal hypothesis test. The setup:

Null hypothesis \(H_0\): \(\theta = 0\) — the CTAs perform equally.
Alternative hypothesis \(H_1\): \(\theta \neq 0\) — one CTA is better.

The goal is to decide whether our observed \(\hat\theta\) is surprising enough under \(H_0\) to count as evidence against it. We do this by standardizing:
\[z = \frac{\hat\theta - 0}{SE(\hat\theta)}\]
Why standardize? Under \(H_0\) we know (by the CLT) that \(\hat\theta\) is approximately \(N(0, SE^2)\). Dividing by \(SE\) transforms it into a standard Normal \(N(0,1)\). We then know exactly how surprising any value of \(z\) is under the null — we just look it up in the standard Normal table (or let the computer do it).
You might hear this called a t-test. The name comes from the classical setting where the raw data are assumed to be Normal and the variance is estimated — the test statistic then follows an exact \(t\)-distribution, not a Normal. Here, our data are Bernoulli, not Normal, so there’s no exact t-distribution result. We’re relying on the CLT for approximate Normality, which technically makes this a z-test. In practice, for large samples the \(t\)-distribution with many degrees of freedom is nearly identical to the standard Normal, so the distinction barely matters — but it’s worth understanding why.


Code
z_stat  = theta_hat / se_analytical
p_value = 2 * (1 - stats.norm.cdf(abs(z_stat)))   # two-sided

print(f"Point estimate  θ̂  = {theta_hat:.4f}")
print(f"Standard error  SE  = {se_analytical:.5f}")
print(f"z-statistic         = {z_stat:.4f}")
print(f"p-value             = {p_value:.4f}")


Point estimate  θ̂  = 0.0250
Standard error  SE  = 0.01825
z-statistic         = 1.3695
p-value             = 0.1708







The observed z-statistic falls well into the rejection region, giving strong evidence against the null hypothesis.











Table 1: Two-Sample z-Test Summary





Quantity
Value




Point estimate (θ̂)
0.0250


Standard error
0.01825


z-statistic
1.3695


p-value
0.1708








With \(z \approx\) 1.37 and \(p \approx\) 0.1708, the result is statistically significant at the conventional \(\alpha = 0.05\) threshold. In other words, if the two CTAs truly performed identically, we would observe a difference this large or larger only about 17.1% of the time — unlikely enough that we reject \(H_0\) and conclude that CTA A converts better.

title: Does Word Choice Matter? Running an A/B Test on a Newsletter Sign-Up
text:
The T-Test as a Regression
There’s a beautiful and practical equivalence hiding in the background: the two-sample test we just ran is mathematically identical to a simple linear regression. Seeing this connection deepens intuition and points toward a much more flexible framework.
Stack the CTA A and CTA B observations into one dataset. Each row has an outcome \(Y_i \in \{0, 1\}\) and a group indicator \(D_i\) (1 for CTA A, 0 for CTA B). Fit:
\[Y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 D_i + \varepsilon_i\]
The coefficient \(\beta_0\) is the expected outcome when \(D_i = 0\) — that’s \(\pi_B\). The coefficient \(\beta_0 + \beta_1\) is the expected outcome when \(D_i = 1\) — that’s \(\pi_A\). So \(\beta_1 = \pi_A - \pi_B = \theta\), and the OLS estimate \(\hat\beta_1\) is numerically the same as \(\hat\theta = \bar{X}_A - \bar{X}_B\).


Code
from scipy.stats import t as t_dist

# Stack the data
Y = np.concatenate([A, B])
D = np.concatenate([np.ones(n), np.zeros(n)])

# OLS by hand (or use scipy/statsmodels)
import statsmodels.api as sm
X_design = sm.add_constant(D)
model    = sm.OLS(Y, X_design).fit()

beta0, beta1 = model.params
se_b1        = model.bse[1]
t_stat       = model.tvalues[1]
p_reg        = model.pvalues[1]

print(f"β̂₀ (≈ π_B)  = {beta0:.4f}  [true π_B = {pi_B}]")
print(f"β̂₁ (≈ θ)   = {beta1:.4f}  [true θ  = 0.04]")
print(f"SE(β̂₁)      = {se_b1:.5f}")
print(f"t-statistic  = {t_stat:.4f}")
print(f"p-value      = {p_reg:.4f}")


β̂₀ (≈ π_B)  = 0.1990  [true π_B = 0.18]
β̂₁ (≈ θ)   = 0.0250  [true θ  = 0.04]
SE(β̂₁)      = 0.01826
t-statistic  = 1.3688
p-value      = 0.1712









Table 2: Two-sample z-test vs. OLS Regression: Side-by-Side Comparison






Two-sample z-test
OLS regression




Point estimate
0.0250
0.0250


Standard error
0.01825
0.01826


Test statistic
1.3695
1.3688


p-value
0.1708
0.1712








The point estimates are identical — they must be, by algebra. The standard errors are very close but not exactly equal: OLS assumes a single pooled residual variance across both groups, while our two-sample formula lets each group have its own variance. For reasonably balanced experiments with similar conversion rates the difference is negligible.
Why does this equivalence matter? Because the regression framework scales. Want to control for the day of the week a visitor arrived? Add a day-of-week dummy. Want to test three CTA variants simultaneously? Add another indicator. Want to check whether mobile users respond differently to the CTAs? Add an interaction term. All of that falls naturally out of regression, and the inference works exactly as it did here.

title: Does Word Choice Matter? Running an A/B Test on a Newsletter Sign-Up
text:
The Problem with Peeking
So far we’ve run our experiment, waited for all 1,000 visitors per group to arrive, and then looked at the result. But what if your boss is impatient? What if she says: “Check the numbers after every 100 visitors — and if it’s significant, we stop early and call a winner.”
This practice is called peeking, and it is one of the most common mistakes in A/B testing. Here’s why it’s a problem.
A single test at \(\alpha = 0.05\) has a 5% chance of falsely rejecting \(H_0\) when the null is actually true (a Type I error). But every time you peek, you get another roll of the dice. Each additional look is a new opportunity to get lucky and see a spuriously significant result. Across 10 peeks, the chance of at least one false positive is much higher than 5%.
Let’s quantify exactly how much higher. We simulate a world where \(H_0\) is true (\(\pi_A = \pi_B = 0.20\), \(\theta = 0\)) and run 10,000 experiments. In each experiment, we peek at the results after 100, 200, …, 1,000 visitors per group.


Code
n_total   = 1_000
peek_at   = np.arange(100, n_total + 1, 100)   # 10 peeks
n_sims    = 10_000
pi_null   = 0.20                               # H0: both groups equal

false_positives = 0

for _ in range(n_sims):
    A_null = rng.binomial(1, pi_null, n_total)
    B_null = rng.binomial(1, pi_null, n_total)

    rejected = False
    for peek in peek_at:
        a_sub = A_null[:peek]
        b_sub = B_null[:peek]
        diff  = a_sub.mean() - b_sub.mean()
        se_p  = np.sqrt(a_sub.mean()*(1-a_sub.mean())/peek +
                        b_sub.mean()*(1-b_sub.mean())/peek)
        if se_p > 0:
            z_p = diff / se_p
            p_p = 2 * (1 - stats.norm.cdf(abs(z_p)))
            if p_p < 0.05:
                rejected = True
                break   # stop the experiment early, as the boss wants

    if rejected:
        false_positives += 1

fpr = false_positives / n_sims
print(f"Nominal false positive rate (single test): 5.0%")
print(f"Empirical false positive rate (10 peeks):  {fpr*100:.1f}%")


Nominal false positive rate (single test): 5.0%
Empirical false positive rate (10 peeks):  19.4%







Peeking dramatically inflates the false positive rate above the nominal 5% level.




The result is stark. A single properly-timed test keeps the false positive rate at its nominal 5%. But peeking 10 times — and stopping as soon as any peek is significant — inflates that rate to around 19%. That means roughly one in four experiments will declare a winner that doesn’t actually exist, simply because we kept looking.
The practical lesson: decide your sample size in advance, run the experiment to completion, and look at the results once. If you genuinely need to peek (e.g., to catch a catastrophic failure), use methods designed for sequential testing — such as sequential probability ratio tests or alpha-spending functions — that maintain valid error control across multiple looks. Classical frequentist tests, run as described in this post, are calibrated for a single look only.

title: Post With Code
text: This is a post with executable code.

title: Poisson Regression and Maximum Likelihood Estimation
text: format: html: toc: true toc-depth: 3 code-fold: show theme: cosmo highlight-style: github self-contained: true

title: Poisson Regression and Maximum Likelihood Estimation
text:
Introduction
Does better software lead to more patents? That is the central question Blueprinty — a small firm that makes blueprint-drafting software tailored for US patent applications — wants its marketing team to answer.
The intuition is simple: Blueprinty’s software streamlines the blueprint-preparation process required by the US Patent Office. If it genuinely improves submission quality, firms using it should receive more patent approvals. To investigate, Blueprinty assembled data on 1,500 mature (non-startup) engineering firms, recording the number of patents each firm earned over the last five years, its geographic region, its age since incorporation, and whether it subscribes to Blueprinty.
The complication is that Blueprinty’s customers are not randomly assigned. Firms that seek out a specialized patent-drafting tool may already be more patent-active, better resourced, or clustered in innovation-dense regions. Any raw comparison of patent counts between customers and non-customers therefore confounds the effect of the software with pre-existing differences between the firms that adopted it. To make a credible marketing claim we need a model that holds firm characteristics constant — which is exactly what Poisson regression, estimated by maximum likelihood, lets us do.

title: Poisson Regression and Maximum Likelihood Estimation
text:
Exploring the Data

Patent Counts by Customer Status









Blueprinty customers average 4.13 patents over the five-year window, compared with 3.47 for non-customers — a gap of about two-thirds of a patent. Both distributions are right-skewed and concentrated in the 0–6 range, which is exactly the kind of count data that motivates a Poisson model. The raw difference looks meaningful, but we cannot attribute it to the software until we check whether customers and non-customers differ along other dimensions.



Regional and Age Differences









Two patterns stand out. First, customers are heavily concentrated in the Northeast: 68% of all customers are Northeast firms, versus only 27% of non-customers. Second, customers are modestly younger on average (26.9 years vs. 26.1 years).
Both differences matter for what follows. If Northeast firms patent more actively — perhaps because that region concentrates innovation hubs — the customer group’s higher patent counts may partly reflect geography, not software. Similarly, if mid-life firms are at peak patenting productivity, the customer age skew could inflate the raw comparison. Controlling for both in a regression lets us isolate the marginal association with Blueprinty.

title: Poisson Regression and Maximum Likelihood Estimation
section: A Simple Poisson Model via Maximum Likelihood
text:
A Simple Poisson Model via Maximum Likelihood

Why Poisson?
Patent counts are non-negative integers — the Normal distribution, which assigns positive probability to negative and fractional values, is a poor structural fit. The Poisson distribution is defined on \(\{0, 1, 2, \ldots\}\), has a single positive parameter \(\lambda\) equal to both its mean and variance, and arises naturally from models of events occurring at a constant rate. It is the textbook starting point for count outcomes like patent awards.


Deriving the Log-Likelihood
Each firm’s count \(Y_i\) is modeled as an independent draw from \(\text{Poisson}(\lambda)\):
\[
f(Y_i \mid \lambda) = \frac{e^{-\lambda}\,\lambda^{Y_i}}{Y_i!}
\]
The joint likelihood for \(n\) independent observations is the product of the individual densities:
\[
L(\lambda \mid \mathbf{Y}) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \frac{e^{-\lambda}\,\lambda^{Y_i}}{Y_i!}
\]
Taking the natural log converts the product into a tractable sum:
\[
\ell(\lambda) = \sum_{i=1}^n \left(-\lambda + Y_i \log \lambda - \log(Y_i!)\right)
= -n\lambda + \log(\lambda)\!\sum_{i=1}^n Y_i - \sum_{i=1}^n \log(Y_i!)
\]
Analytical MLE. Differentiating with respect to \(\lambda\) and setting equal to zero:
\[
\frac{d\ell}{d\lambda} = -n + \frac{\sum_i Y_i}{\lambda} = 0
\implies \hat{\lambda}_{\text{MLE}} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_i Y_i = \bar{Y}
\]
The MLE is simply the sample mean — sensible, since \(\lambda\) is the population mean of a Poisson.


Coding and Plotting the Log-Likelihood


Code
Y = d['patents'].values

def poisson_loglikelihood(lam, Y):
    """Log-likelihood for Y ~ Poisson(lambda)."""
    if lam <= 0:
        return -np.inf
    return np.sum(-lam + Y * np.log(lam) - gammaln(Y + 1))











The curve has a single smooth peak. The maximum sits exactly at \(\hat{\lambda} = \bar{Y}\), as predicted analytically. Values of \(\lambda\) that are too small assign too little probability to the many firms with multiple patents; values that are too large over-predict counts for the zero-and-one firms. The MLE balances these competing forces.


Numerical Confirmation


Code
result_simple = minimize(
    lambda x: -poisson_loglikelihood(x[0], Y),
    x0=[3.0],
    method='Nelder-Mead'
)
lambda_mle = result_simple.x[0]
print(f"Numerical MLE:  λ̂ = {lambda_mle:.6f}")
print(f"Sample mean:    Ȳ = {Y.mean():.6f}")


Numerical MLE:  λ̂ = 3.684668
Sample mean:    Ȳ = 3.684667


The numerical optimizer and the closed-form formula agree to six decimal places — as expected, since the log-likelihood has a unique global maximum and the analytical solution pins it down exactly.

title: Poisson Regression and Maximum Likelihood Estimation
text:
Poisson Regression

From a Single \(\lambda\) to Firm-Specific Rates
The simple model treats every firm as exchangeable. To use our covariate information we extend it so that each firm \(i\) has its own rate:
\[
Y_i \sim \text{Poisson}(\lambda_i), \qquad \lambda_i = \exp(X_i'\beta)
\]
The exponential link is essential: the linear predictor \(X_i'\beta\) can take any real value, but \(\lambda_i\) must be strictly positive. Exponentiating guarantees positivity. Each coefficient \(\beta_j\) is the log of a multiplicative factor on the expected count — a one-unit increase in \(x_j\) multiplies \(\lambda_i\) by \(e^{\beta_j}\).


The Regression Log-Likelihood
Replacing the scalar \(\lambda\) with \(\exp(X_i'\beta)\):
\[
\ell(\beta) = \sum_{i=1}^n \left[-\exp(X_i'\beta) + Y_i\,(X_i'\beta) - \log(Y_i!)\right]
\]


Code
def poisson_regression_loglikelihood(beta, Y, X):
    """Log-likelihood for Poisson regression Y ~ Poisson(exp(X @ beta))."""
    eta = np.clip(X @ beta, -500, 500)   # guard against overflow
    return np.sum(-np.exp(eta) + Y * eta - gammaln(Y + 1))




Building the Design Matrix


Code
# South is the omitted (reference) region
region_dummies = pd.get_dummies(d['region'], drop_first=False).drop(columns=['South'])

X_df = pd.concat([
    pd.Series(np.ones(len(d)), name='intercept'),
    d[['age']],
    (d['age'] ** 2).rename('age_sq'),
    region_dummies,
    d[['iscustomer']]
], axis=1)

X = X_df.values.astype(float)
print(f"Design matrix: {X.shape[0]} firms x {X.shape[1]} columns")
print(f"Columns: {X_df.columns.tolist()}")


Design matrix: 1500 firms x 8 columns
Columns: ['intercept', 'age', 'age_sq', 'Midwest', 'Northeast', 'Northwest', 'Southwest', 'iscustomer']


Why include age-squared? Patenting activity is unlikely to increase linearly with firm age throughout the entire life cycle. Young firms are still building R&D pipelines; established mid-life firms often reach peak patent productivity; very old firms may have diversified away from core innovation. A quadratic in age captures this hump-shaped pattern without imposing a strong assumption about where the inflection lies.
Why drop one region dummy? The five region indicators sum to 1 for every observation — identical to the intercept column. Including all five would make the design matrix rank-deficient (perfect collinearity). Dropping South makes it the reference category: every other regional coefficient measures the difference in log-expected patents relative to a Southern firm, all else equal.


Estimating \(\hat\beta\) with scipy.optimize


Code
# Warm-start from GLM to ensure numerical stability
glm_init = sm.GLM(Y, X, family=sm.families.Poisson()).fit()

result_reg = minimize(
    lambda b: -poisson_regression_loglikelihood(b, Y, X),
    x0=glm_init.params.copy(),
    method='L-BFGS-B',
    options={'maxiter': 10_000, 'ftol': 1e-12}
)
print(f"Optimizer converged: {result_reg.success}")


Optimizer converged: True


Standard errors from the Hessian. Near the MLE the log-likelihood surface is locally quadratic, so the second-derivative matrix (Hessian) captures how sharply peaked it is in each parameter direction. A sharper peak means less uncertainty. The asymptotic covariance matrix of \(\hat\beta\) is the inverse of the negative Hessian:
\[
\widehat{\operatorname{Var}}(\hat{\beta}) \approx \left[-\nabla^2 \ell(\hat{\beta})\right]^{-1}
\]


Code
def neg_ll(b):
    return -poisson_regression_loglikelihood(b, Y, X)

# Approximate Hessian via finite differences
eps = 1e-5
n_p = len(result_reg.x)
H   = np.zeros((n_p, n_p))
for i in range(n_p):
    def grad_i(b, i=i):   # capture i by value
        return approx_fprime(b, neg_ll, eps)[i]
    H[i] = approx_fprime(result_reg.x, grad_i, eps)

se_optim = np.sqrt(np.diag(np.linalg.inv(H)))




Comparison with statsmodels GLM


Code
glm_fit = sm.GLM(Y, X, family=sm.families.Poisson()).fit()





    

Table 1: Poisson Regression Coefficients
Manual optim() vs. statsmodels GLM

optim()
statsmodels GLM

The coefficient estimates from optim() and sm.GLM() are identical to four decimal places — a reassuring sanity check. The standard errors are very close but not exactly equal: sm.GLM() uses the analytically computed expected Fisher information matrix, while our approach approximates the Hessian numerically via finite differences. This small discretization error explains the minor discrepancy, but it does not change any substantive conclusion.


Interpreting the Coefficients



    

Table 2: GLM Coefficient Estimates
With multiplicative effects exp(β̂)

Because the model is log-linear, each \(\hat\beta_j\) is the log of a multiplicative factor on \(\hat\lambda_i\); exponentiating gives the rate multiplier per unit change in \(x_j\):

Age (\(\hat\beta = 0.149\), \(e^{\hat\beta} \approx 1.16\)): expected patents rise with firm age — each additional year multiplies the rate by ~1.16, all else equal.
Age² (\(\hat\beta = -0.003\)): the negative quadratic confirms a concave life-cycle pattern — patenting accelerates early but eventually levels off as firms mature.
Regional dummies: relative to the South, regional differences are modest and within roughly one standard error of zero across all regions.
Is Customer (\(\hat\beta = 0.208\), \(e^{\hat\beta} \approx 1.23\)): the key finding. Controlling for age and region, Blueprinty customers are expected to receive ~23% more patents than otherwise identical non-customers. The coefficient is more than six standard errors from zero.

title: Poisson Regression and Maximum Likelihood Estimation
text:
The Effect of Blueprinty’s Software

Average Marginal Effect via Counterfactual Prediction
The 23% multiplicative lift from the customer coefficient is harder to communicate than “X additional patents.” Moreover, the absolute effect varies across firms — a high-rate firm gains more patents in absolute terms from the same proportional boost than a low-rate firm does. To get a single interpretable number we use counterfactual prediction: predict each firm’s expected patent count with and without customer status, holding every other covariate at its observed value.


Code
beta_hat = result_reg.x

# Counterfactual 0: every firm set to non-customer
X0 = X.copy(); X0[:, -1] = 0
# Counterfactual 1: every firm set to customer
X1 = X.copy(); X1[:, -1] = 1

y_hat_0 = np.exp(X0 @ beta_hat)
y_hat_1 = np.exp(X1 @ beta_hat)

ate = np.mean(y_hat_1 - y_hat_0)
print(f"Average treatment effect: {ate:.4f} additional patents over 5 years")


Average treatment effect: 0.7928 additional patents over 5 years











The estimated average treatment effect is 0.793 additional patents over the five-year window. The distribution of firm-level effects is right-skewed — firms with higher base patent rates gain more in absolute terms — but the effect is positive for virtually every firm in the sample.


What This Analysis Does — and Does Not — Establish
The evidence is consistent with Blueprinty’s marketing claim: after controlling for firm age and region, customers patent at a meaningfully higher rate, gaining roughly 0.79 additional patents per five years. For a firm whose competitiveness depends on intellectual property protection, that is a non-trivial gain.
That said, this is an observational study, not a randomized experiment. Firms self-selected into Blueprinty’s software, and we cannot rule out every unobserved confounder — quality of in-house patent counsel, R&D budget, corporate culture around IP, or the intrinsic patentability of the firm’s technology. Any of these could partially explain the patent-count gap even after controlling for age and region.
The honest conclusion: the data are consistent with Blueprinty’s software improving patent success, and the association survives controls for the most salient confounders. A causal claim would require either a randomized rollout or a credible natural experiment generating as-good-as-random variation in adoption.
